clinical_history: This ECG from a 56 year old man who was found unresponsive is most consistent with which ONE of the following clinical scenarios?
diagnosis: The ECG shows atrial fibrillation (AF) with an average ventricular response of about 100/min.  Nonspecific ST-T changes are present with QT prolongation (about 400 ms). Of special note are the prominent Osborn waves, seen at the J junction (at end of the QRS and beginning of ST segment). Not including the J waves, the QRS duration (width) is at the upper limits of normal or slightly prolonged. The patient had severe hypothermia (rectal temperature of (28.4 degrees C or 83.1 F). Serum potassium was borderline low (3.3 mEq/L) and serum calcium was normal. The AF converted spontaneously back to sinus rhythm.  The J wave of hypothermia has been attributed to altered action potential characteristics in epicardial cells leading to a voltage gradient between the endocardium and the outer ventricular layers at the time the J wave is inscribed. (See: Yan G-X, Antzelevitch C. Cellular basis for the electrocardiographic J wave. Circulation. 1996; 93:372379.)  Renal failure would be associated with signs of hyperkalemia, not seen here. Tricyclic antidepressant overdose is typically associated with resting sinus tachycardia, a wide QRS and a long QT.  ECG findings in myxedema coma would include sinus bradycardia and diffusely low QRS voltage. Hypercalcemia is associated with QT (ST segment portion) shortening.
